title: Emergency Care: Cat Hit by Car and Dragging Leg | Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat was hit by a car and is dragging a leg, this is a high-priority medical emergency. Follow these triage steps and seek immediate care.
content_html:
What is it?

A cat dragging its leg after being hit by a car (Motor Vehicle Accident) typically indicates severe trauma. This presentation often suggests a complete bone fracture, a joint dislocation (luxation), or significant nerve damage, such as a brachial plexus avulsion where the nerves connecting the limb to the spine are torn. Additionally, dragging a limb can be a sign of a spinal injury or a pelvic fracture.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

This situation is a critical medical emergency. Even if your cat appears conscious or alert, the force of a vehicle impact often causes life-threatening internal injuries, such as a ruptured bladder, internal bleeding, or a collapsed lung (pneumothorax), which may not be immediately visible. Delaying treatment can lead to irreversible shock or permanent loss of limb function.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If it is safe to do so without delaying your trip to the clinic, taking a quick photo of the limb's positioning and any visible wounds can be incredibly helpful for the veterinary team. A photo allows the triage nurse to assess the 'knuckling' of the paw, the angle of a potential fracture, and the presence of skin tears or 'road rash' before they even begin a physical exam. This visual data helps the team prepare the necessary stabilization equipment and pain management before you arrive.

faqs.question: Can I wait a few hours to see if my cat starts walking?
faqs.answer: No. Dragging a leg after a trauma indicates serious injury. Internal bleeding or shock can worsen rapidly, and early intervention is vital for the best chance of recovery.

faqs.question: Should I try to splint the leg at home?
faqs.answer: No, do not attempt to splint the leg. This is often very painful and can cause further damage to the bone or surrounding tissues. Instead, gently place your cat in a secure carrier or box lined with soft towels to minimize movement.

faqs.question: Does dragging the leg mean it has to be amputated?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. While some nerve injuries or severe fractures may lead to amputation, many cats can recover through surgery or physical therapy depending on the specific diagnosis made by a veterinarian.
